summary: In this interview, moral philosopher Mary Midgley critiques the misuse of evolutionary theory as a modern creation myth. She warns against scientists who unknowingly act as ideological gurus, promoting one-sided narratives of savage competition or inevitable progress. Midgley also argues against reducing humans to machines and treating consciousness as a mere byproduct, emphasizing instead the vital role of imagination in shaping scientific thought and human meaning.
themes: The role of imagination in science

principles.statement: Evolution is not an escalator of continuous progress.
principles.rationale: The belief that evolution guarantees continuous improvement or growth is a myth. In nature, things do not grow indefinitely; they grow until they reach an appropriate size.
principles.source_quote: the idea that growth—for instance, economic growth—is natural and required, is a mythical idea. This can’t be right, because things do not grow indefinitely in nature; they grow until they’re big enough.

principles.statement: Organisms are fundamentally cooperative, not just savagely competitive.
principles.rationale: Life relies on immense cooperation at a cellular level. Furthermore, evolutionary competition often means finding a new niche or food source, not necessarily fighting another organism.
principles.source_quote: this is a fantasy about how life was made, because organisms cooperate constantly. The little bits in our cells were originally separate organisms which settled down to work together.

principles.statement: Consciousness is a cause, not merely an effect.
principles.rationale: Mental states actively affect physical states. It is unrealistic to treat consciousness as an 'epiphenomenon' floating on top of physical processes without influencing them.
principles.source_quote: it really is a piece of gratuitous metaphysics, this idea that consciousness is just an effect and never a cause.

principles.statement: Imagination determines the shape of our scientific theories.
principles.rationale: Big scientific concepts do not correspond directly to raw facts; they are imaginative ways of assembling facts. Foundational scientific ideas, like atomic theory, often originate in poetry and philosophy.
principles.source_quote: The shape of our imagination determines the kind of scientific theories we can hold, doesn’t it?

mental_models.name: Science as Mythmaking
mental_models.description: The concept that major scientific theories (like evolution) function as modern creation myths, providing cultures with stories to explain why things are the way they are. Scientists often unknowingly act as mythmakers when they extend their theories into overarching ideologies.
mental_models.example: Treating Darwin's theory of evolution as a story of either inevitable societal progress or savage, laissez-faire economic competition.

mental_models.name: The Machine Power Fantasy
mental_models.description: The tendency to view human beings as machines or computers. This acts as a power fantasy that allows people to believe complex human problems can be easily engineered or fixed with physical tools, avoiding the frightening reality of dealing with internal feelings.
mental_models.example: Psychiatrists hoping to cure complex mental illnesses entirely with physical pills, treating the human mind like a car engine that just needs the right spanner.

heuristics: Big concepts don’t correspond directly with facts; they are ways of assembling facts.
You can’t use raw feeling directly; it must be processed, brought into relation with the rest of your life, and made into thought.

signature_quotes.text: The shape of our imagination determines the kind of scientific theories we can hold, doesn’t it?
signature_quotes.context: Discussing how the atomic theory originated from the poetic and philosophical writings of Lucretius and the Greek atomists.

signature_quotes.text: What has often happened is scientists who are scrupulous in the main body of their work, when they get to the last chapter, they have a holiday, you see.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how specialized scientists often unknowingly slip into mythmaking and ideology at the end of their books.

signature_quotes.text: It really is a piece of gratuitous metaphysics, this idea that consciousness is just an effect and never a cause.
signature_quotes.context: Criticizing the medical and psychiatric orthodoxy that attempts to treat mental illness purely through physical means.

signature_quotes.text: Ideology is boiling out of books that get sold as science, because the book is supposed to be a scientific book, whereas the person is really acting as a guru or a prophet who should be judged on the merit of his prophecies.
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing scientists who present one-sided stories about human nature with the unearned authority of impartial science.

signature_quotes.text: The visions of the imagination are a crucial and real part of human life, and what is operating there is real.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the human concept of God and the mistake of trying to amputate it from human experience.

anti_patterns.description: Treating Darwinism purely as a story of savage competition.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the massive amount of cooperation required for organisms to exist at all, and falsely equates evolutionary success with fighting, when it often just means finding a new niche.

Because their education is highly specialized and lacks philosophy or history, scientists often mistake their one-sided metaphors for impartial facts, presenting ideology under the guise of scientific authority.

anti_patterns.description: Treating humans as machines that can be easily engineered.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It exaggerates human power and knowledge, leading to unrealistic medical approaches (like trying to cure all mental illness with pills) because people are frightened of engaging imaginatively with complex feelings.

anti_patterns.description: Dismissing religion aggressively as disproved rubbish.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the historical and cultural context of believers, making it impossible for them to accept new scientific facts, and unnecessarily turns science and religion into competing tribes.
